skill.name: azure-messaging
skill.description: Troubleshoot Azure Event Hubs and Azure Service Bus SDK issues across Python, Java, JavaScript, and .NET. Use it for connection failures, authentication errors, AMQP issues, lock loss, logging, retry settings, checkpointing, and message processing problems.
skill.summary: Diagnose Azure Event Hubs and Service Bus SDK problems with language-specific troubleshooting guidance.
skill.author: microsoft

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many command, network, weak-crypto, and heuristic hits, but review found documentation-only examples and Microsoft or Azure reference links rather than executable skill code. The critical heuristic is dismissed because there is no prompt injection, obfuscation, credential exfiltration, or automatic command execution. Low risk remains because the skill suggests user-run diagnostic commands and verbose logging that can expose operational details if shared carelessly.

low_findings.title: User-Run Diagnostic Commands in Documentation
low_findings.description: The command findings are false positives for execution risk because they are markdown examples and inline command names. They still have low operational risk because users may run curl, nslookup, netstat, tcpdump, node redirection, or environment variable commands during troubleshooting.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The referenced lines are clearly markdown troubleshooting examples, not code that the skill executes. The remaining risk is user behavior, such as running diagnostic commands in production without review.

low_findings.title: Verbose Logging May Expose Operational Details
low_findings.description: Several guides recommend DEBUG or verbose logging and asking users to include debug logs when filing issues. This is legitimate troubleshooting guidance, but logs can include namespace names, addresses, timing data, and message metadata that should be reviewed before sharing.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The logging guidance is explicit and useful, but the files do not instruct users to publish secrets. The concern is credible but limited to data hygiene around debug output.

low_findings.title: Browser Storage Used Only for Debug Configuration
low_findings.description: The browser storage alert is a low-risk true positive for localStorage access, but the value only enables Azure SDK debug logging. No credential, token, or user data storage is shown.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Line 30 writes a debug namespace string to localStorage, which confirms browser storage access. The surrounding context shows logging configuration only, so impact is low.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Alerts Are Substring False Positives
low_findings.description: The weak cryptography hits do not show DES, MD5, SHA1, or other cryptographic API use. The flagged lines contain ordinary documentation text such as descriptions, package names, or dead letter description fields.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Manual review of the exact flagged lines found no cryptographic operations. The detector appears to have matched substrings inside benign words or SDK documentation text.

dangerous_patterns.title: Markdown Command Examples Misclassified as External Execution
dangerous_patterns.description: Backticks and fenced blocks contain MCP tool names, shell snippets, logging flags, and language examples. They are not invoked automatically by the skill and do not form a command injection path.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The exact locations are markdown documentation, and this skill has no script or runtime file that executes them. The static command pattern is real text but false positive for automatic execution.

dangerous_patterns.title: Hardcoded URLs Are Documentation and Azure Endpoint References
dangerous_patterns.description: Network findings point to Microsoft Learn, Azure SDK GitHub repositories, Azure status, and placeholder Azure endpoints. No unauthorized external callback or data exfiltration behavior was found.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: All reviewed URLs are vendor documentation, GitHub source references, Azure status, or placeholder service endpoints. No code sends credentials or local data to those URLs.

content.value_statement: Azure messaging failures are hard to diagnose when errors differ by SDK language and service. This skill gives targeted troubleshooting steps for Event Hubs and Service Bus clients.
actual_capabilities: Maps Event Hubs and Service Bus SDK errors to likely causes and fixes.
Provides language-specific guidance for Python, Java, JavaScript, and .NET SDKs.
Explains AMQP connectivity, firewall ports, WebSocket fallback, and private endpoint checks.
Guides debugging for message locks, sessions, retries, batching, and checkpoint stores.
Shows how to enable SDK logging and collect useful context for support cases.
limitations: It does not create or configure Azure resources for the user.
It does not replace live Azure monitoring, metrics, or service health checks.
It relies on the user to provide accurate SDK version, error, and topology details.
It does not guarantee that referenced SDK guidance is the latest without documentation lookup.

use_cases.title: Resolve Event Hubs Consumer Failures
use_cases.description: Diagnose consumers that stop receiving messages, lose ownership, or fail checkpointing across supported SDK languages.

use_cases.title: Investigate Service Bus Lock Issues
use_cases.description: Find causes for MessageLockLost, SessionLockLost, auto-renewal problems, and dead letter processing issues.

use_cases.title: Prepare Messaging Support Evidence
use_cases.description: Collect SDK logs, network checks, configuration details, and reproduction context before opening a support issue.

prompt_templates.title: Diagnose a Basic SDK Error
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner troubleshooting for a single error message.
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me troubleshoot this Azure Messaging SDK error. Service: [Event Hubs or Service Bus]. Language: [Python, Java, JavaScript, or .NET]. Error message: [paste error].

prompt_templates.title: Review Connection and Authentication
prompt_templates.scenario: Intermediate check for connection, firewall, and identity problems.
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this Azure messaging connection issue. Include checks for credentials, RBAC, AMQP ports, WebSocket fallback, firewall rules, and private endpoints. Context: [describe environment].

prompt_templates.title: Debug Message Processing Behavior
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced analysis of processing, lock, retry, and batching problems.
prompt_templates.prompt: Analyze why my Azure messaging processor behaves incorrectly. Include likely SDK causes, configuration checks, and safe logging steps. Details: [service, SDK version, concurrency, prefetch, lock duration, symptoms].

prompt_templates.title: Build a Support-Ready Triage Plan
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced incident triage with evidence collection.
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a support-ready triage plan for this Azure Messaging incident. Include evidence to collect, diagnostic commands to review before running, log redaction guidance, and likely remediation paths. Incident details: [details].

output_examples.input: A JavaScript Event Hubs consumer stops receiving messages after scaling out.
output: Likely causes include partition ownership churn, high CPU, or recovery after transient AMQP errors.
Check DEBUG logs around the stop time, partition count, instance count, and checkpoint store behavior.
Expect temporary 412 precondition messages during ownership negotiation; persistent churn needs deeper review.

output_examples.input: A Service Bus receiver reports MessageLockLost before the configured lock expires.
output: The lock can be lost because of AMQP link detach, idle timeout, or processing that exceeds renewal limits.
Review network stability, auto-lock renewal settings, processing time, and whether clients are reused as singletons.
Use verbose logs only after planning redaction for namespace names and message metadata.

output_examples.input: A Python Event Hubs client cannot connect from a restricted network.
output: Verify the fully qualified namespace, credentials, RBAC role, and firewall allow list.
Check AMQP port access and consider WebSocket transport over port 443 when direct AMQP is blocked.
Confirm private endpoint, subnet, proxy, and DNS behavior before changing retry settings.

best_practices: Start with the exact SDK language, version, service, entity type, and full error message.
Review diagnostic commands and log settings before running them in production environments.
Redact connection strings, SAS tokens, namespace names, and message data before sharing logs.
anti_patterns: Do not create a new messaging client for every send or receive operation.
Do not treat all lock-loss errors as slow processing without checking link detach and network behavior.
Do not paste unredacted DEBUG logs, connection strings, or SAS tokens into support requests.

faq.question: Does this skill support both Event Hubs and Service Bus?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes troubleshooting references for both services across Python, Java, JavaScript, and .NET SDKs.

faq.question: Can it create queues, topics, namespaces, or event hubs?
faq.answer: No. It focuses on SDK troubleshooting and points resource creation tasks to other Azure preparation workflows.

faq.question: Can it help with AMQP connection failures?
faq.answer: Yes. It covers ports, WebSocket fallback, firewall checks, private endpoints, proxies, and transient disconnect behavior.

faq.question: Will it automatically run diagnostic commands?
faq.answer: No. Commands in the references are examples for users to review and run only when appropriate.

faq.question: Does it handle message lock and session lock errors?
faq.answer: Yes. It covers lock loss, auto-renewal, session behavior, idle disconnects, and processing-time causes.

faq.question: How should I share logs safely?
faq.answer: Enable verbose logs only when needed, then redact credentials, namespace names, message bodies, and customer data before sharing.
